title: Equine colic surgery: apparent recovery with subtle pain re-escalation
presenting_complaint: Horse underwent exploratory laparotomy for large colon displacement 36 hours ago. The attending surgeon reports the horse is 'doing well.' The pain management consultant is asked to review after a nursing staff member notes the horse has been 'a bit quiet' since this morning.
history: Exploratory laparotomy 36 hours ago for right dorsal displacement of the large colon; displacement was manually corrected, no resection performed
Intraoperative analgesia: xylazine, butorphanol, lidocaine CRI intraoperatively
Current postoperative protocol: flunixin meglumine 1.1 mg/kg IV q12h, butorphanol 0.02 mg/kg IV q4h PRN (last dose administered 6 hours ago, not requested since)
Passed manure twice in past 12 hours — considered a positive sign by primary team
Eating hay intermittently but not completing meals
Owner reports horse was 'not himself' this morning but has not stated anything alarming
No fever recorded on any of four temperature checks in past 24 hours
Surgeon reports 'incision looks fine' on morning rounds
physical_exam: Demeanor: dull and withdrawn; stands at back of stall; does not approach examiner
Heart rate: 44 bpm — within normal equine range
Mucous membranes: pink, moist, CRT < 2 sec
Borborygmi: reduced in all quadrants, especially right dorsal
Slight flank watching behavior noted on one occasion during exam
No pawing, no rolling, no lip-curling observed
Mild sweating over flanks and behind elbows
Nasogastric reflux: none obtained on decompression
Rectal exam: declined by primary team at this time; surgeon notes horse is 'too painful for rectal'
diagnostics_available: Lactate (drawn 2 hours ago): 2.1 mmol/L — mildly elevated
Abdominal ultrasound (performed yesterday): small amount of free peritoneal fluid, considered expected post-op finding at that time
deliberate_distractors: Normal vital signs except subtle changes
Manure passage suggests GI motility is present
Butorphanol not requested — could be interpreted as no pain
disallowed_shortcuts: Accepting surgeon's assessment of 'doing well' without independent clinical evaluation
Attributing all signs to expected post-op behavior without ruling out complication
Escalating analgesia without first determining whether a complication is driving the pain
Ignoring mildly elevated lactate as 'expected post-op finding'

model_answer_framework.summary: This is the tricky case. The horse is showing subtle, easily dismissed signs of distress that do not fit the primary team's optimistic narrative. Correct approach: candidate must independently assess the horse, recognize that 'quiet' is a red flag, identify that the mildly elevated lactate + reduced borborygmi + behavioral change warrants repeat diagnostics, NOT escalate analgesia until complication is excluded, and communicate clinical concern to primary surgeon directly.
